entry.title: Why the SAP Integrated Toolchain Is the Future of Digital Transformation
entry.content:
Over the past year, I've published the 3 HOW’S blogs exploring how Enterprise Architects can unlock strategic value from the SAP toolchain. The first went deep on "WalkMe" as a strategic extension of architectural governance. The second explored how SAP "Signavio" bridges the gap between business process design and IT implementation. The third brought these perspectives together with "LeanIX" to show how the three tools create a comprehensive EA ecosystem  from strategic planning through execution and adoption.

The response from the SAP Community exceeded my expectations. The blogs collectively reached thousands of readers across the ANZ region and globally, and the conversations that followed were some of the richest professional exchanges I've had. But the questions I received most consistently weren't about the individual tools. They were about the connections: How does Signavio actually synchronize with LeanIX in practice? What does the Cloud ALM–Tricentis integration look like when you turn it on? How do WalkMe and Enable Now divide responsibilities when you have both deployed? Which integration should a team activate first if they're just getting started?

Those questions deserve proper answers  not just a paragraph or two, but dedicated, detailed exploration. That's what this series delivers. Over the days, I will go deep on each of the most impactful integrations in the SAP Integrated Toolchain: what each integration does, why it matters strategically, how to activate it, and what it looks like in practice with real-world scenarios drawn from customer programs I've been involved in across the region.

This is the integration guide I wish had existed when I started working with the toolchain. I hope it becomes the resource you reach for when your organization is ready to close the Integration Gap.

The Integration Gap: A Problem More Common Than You Think

Let me describe a scenario that I encounter, in some variation, in almost every customer engagement I'm part of across the Globe.

An organization has invested heavily in their SAP integrated toolchain. They have LeanIX for Enterprise Architecture Management, with a well-maintained application landscape covering hundreds of fact sheets. They have SAP Signavio for Business Process Management, with documented process models covering their core end-to-end processes. They have SAP Cloud ALM running as their implementation backbone, managing project tasks, test plans, and deployment workflows. They have Tricentis handling automated test execution. They have WalkMe providing in-application guidance to their SAP users. Some of them also have SAP Enable Now managing their learning content library.

Every one of these tools, evaluated independently, is performing well. The teams using each tool are competent and engaged. The data in each system is reasonably accurate and current.

And yet the transformation is struggling. Timelines are slipping. Costs are running over. User adoption after go-live is lower than projected. The post-implementation audit will eventually conclude  as they almost always do  that the root cause was "communication and coordination challenges between workstreams."

What that conclusion won't capture is the specific, avoidable mechanism behind those challenges: the tools that should have been talking to each other weren't. The Signavio process designs that should have automatically populated Cloud ALM implementation structures were being manually transcribed by a project manager  a process that introduced errors, took days instead of minutes, and fell behind every time a process was updated. The Tricentis test cases that should have been linked to the Cloud ALM tasks they validated were floating in an unconnected test management environment, making impact analysis for scope changes a manual, error-prone exercise. The Enable Now training content that should have been built on synchronized Signavio process structures was being created from static screenshots taken three months before go-live — content that was already outdated by the time users saw it.

The integrations that would have prevented all of this existed. SAP had built them. They simply hadn't been activated.

This is what I call the "Integration Gap"  the distance between the toolchain an organization has licensed and the toolchain it is actually operating. And in my experience, it is the single most common source of avoidable transformation cost, delay, and risk in SAP programs today.

 

Real-World Case Study: The Cost of Disconnected Integrations

In one large-scale RISE S/4HANA rollout program case studies I was involved in, the project team had both Signavio and Cloud ALM deployed but not integrated. A dedicated resource was spending significant time each week manually synchronizing process updates between the two systems , transcribing model changes, updating task descriptions, and notifying the right people.

When the Signavio–Cloud ALM integration was activated mid-program, the manual effort dropped Dramatically. Process updates in Signavio propagated automatically into Cloud ALM within minutes. The resource was redeployed to higher-value work. The integration paid for the effort of activating it within the first fortnight.

This pattern repeats across every integration in the toolchain. The connections are not just efficiency gains  they are risk reduction mechanisms that the program is currently absorbing the cost of manually.

What Is the SAP Integrated Toolchain?

The SAP Integrated Toolchain is not a single product, a bundle, or a suite in the traditional sense. It is a deliberately curated ecosystem of best-in-class tools  some built by SAP, some acquired, some partnered  that are connected by a growing set of out-of-the-box integrations designed to support the full lifecycle of a modern SAP transformation program.

The toolchain spans six capability domains that every serious transformation program must address:

 

The Six Domains of the SAP Integrated Toolchain

• Enterprise Architecture Management - SAP LeanIX: application portfolio management, capability mapping, transformation roadmapping, and IT/business alignment

• Business Process Management & Intelligence - SAP Signavio: process design, process mining, conformance checking, governance, and transformation management

•  Application Lifecycle Management - SAP Cloud ALM : implementation orchestration, project management, fit-to-standard workshops, and ongoing operations

•  Digital Adoption & User Enablement - SAP WalkMe & SAP Enable Now: real-time in-application guidance, behavioral analytics, learning content management, and change communication

•  Test Automation & Quality Assurance - SAP by Tricentis: AI-driven continuous testing, change impact analysis, and no-code test automation across SAP and non-SAP systems

•  Data Migration & Quality - Syniti: data profiling, cleansing, migration execution, and post-migration quality monitoring

What separates the SAP Integrated Toolchain from a collection of individually purchased tools is the investment SAP has made in the integrations between them. These are not custom-built connectors that each customer must develop and maintain. They are out-of-the-box, SAP-supported integrations with documented setup processes, configuration guides, and  video walkthroughs published on the LeanIX Academy, SAP Learning, and the SAP Community. They are built to be activated by practitioners, not by developers.

The toolchain spans the entire transformation lifecycle from the earliest strategic planning phases through implementation, testing, go-live, and the continuous improvement cycle that follows. When its integrations are active, it creates what I describe as the Digital Thread: a continuous, unbroken flow of data and context from strategy through to adoption.

Why the Integrations Are the Point

Without Integration: The Siloed Transformation

An Enterprise Architect designs a business capability map in LeanIX. Separately, a process manager documents those same capabilities as BPMN process models in Signavio, without reference to the LeanIX structure. Separately again, an implementation consultant builds project tasks in Cloud ALM, referencing the Signavio models by opening them in a browser tab and reading from them manually. A test manager designs test cases in Tricentis, cross-referencing the Cloud ALM task list and the Signavio diagrams by hand. An enablement team builds training content in Enable Now, working from a PDF export of the Signavio models taken three months earlier. A WalkMe team builds in-application guidance independently, referencing their own documentation.

When a process changes and in a real transformation program, processes change constantly, right up to go-live the update must be manually propagated through every silo. The Signavio model is updated. Someone has to remember to tell the Cloud ALM team. Someone has to tell the Tricentis team which test cases are affected. Someone has to tell the Enable Now team which training modules need revision. Someone has to tell the WalkMe team which walk-thrus need updating. Each handoff is a potential failure point. Each delay is a potential risk.

This is not a hypothetical. This is the default operating model of most SAP transformation programs today.

With Integration: The Connected Transformation

Now consider the same program with the toolchain integrations active.

The LeanIX–Signavio integration means that when a process model is created or updated in Signavio, it automatically creates or updates the corresponding Process Fact Sheet in LeanIX. The applications supporting that process  captured in LeanIX  are available directly in the Signavio modeling environment. Enterprise Architects and process consultants are working from the same data, in their respective tools, without manual synchronization.

The Signavio–Cloud ALM integration means that when a process model is approved in Signavio, it flows directly into the Cloud ALM implementation project structure. Implementation consultants can see the process design  in context  within Cloud ALM, without switching tools. When the process is updated in Signavio, the Cloud ALM team is immediately aware.

The Tricentis–Cloud ALM integration means test cases are managed within the same project context as the implementation tasks they validate. Tricentis's AI-driven change impact analysis identifies exactly which test cases need to run for any given configuration change, reducing test scope by up to 85% while maintaining over 90% business risk coverage.

The Enable Now/WalkMe–Signavio integration means training content is built on process structures synchronized directly from Signavio. When a process changes, Enable Now/Walkme flags the affected content for review immediately not after a human notices the discrepancy.

The result is not just efficiency. It is a fundamentally different quality of governance: changes propagate automatically, impacts surface immediately, and the entire program team operates from a single, consistent version of the truth across every tool.

The Integrations This Series Will Cover

In this Blog Series I've selected the main integrations based on their strategic impact, their frequency of relevance across transformation programs, and the depth of their out-of-the-box capability. Each blog is written to be self-contained  you can read them in sequence or jump directly to the integration most relevant to your current program phase or role.

These  integrations are drawn directly from the S/4 Transformation End-2-End Integrated Toolchain architecture SAP’s own blueprint for how these tools connect to systematically bridge Business and IT Transformation. The diagram below (which you will see referenced throughout this series) shows Cloud ALM at the centre, with Signavio feeding process and requirement content in from the left, LeanIX providing landscape and initiative data from below, Tricentis consuming test cases from the right, WalkMe and Enable Now delivering enablement content on the far right, and Syniti handling data migration at the top left. Every integration in this series traces directly to a connection shown in that architecture.

Each blog will follow a consistent structure: what the integration does, why it matters strategically, how Enterprise Architects and transformation leaders can leverage it, practical activation guidance, and a real-world scenario showing the integration in action. I will also link to the relevant SAP-published setup videos and documentation at the end of each blog.

The Digital Thread: Seeing the Whole System

Before we dive into individual integrations, I want to introduce the concept that will serve as the through-line for the entire series: the Digital Thread.

The Digital Thread is the unbroken flow of data and context that connects every phase of a transformation program from the earliest strategic decisions to execution through implementation, testing, go-live, and the ongoing improvement cycle when the toolchain integrations are active.  It's the difference between a program where information has to be handed off manually between phases and one where information flows automatically across the entire lifecycle.

In a fully integrated toolchain, the Digital Thread looks like this:

The Integrated Transformation Digital Thread

•  Strategic intent is captured in LeanIX  capability models, target architectures, and transformation roadmaps establish what the organization needs to achieve and by when

•  Process designs in Signavio are connected to those capabilities  establishing precisely how each capability will be delivered through specific, documented business processes

•  Those process designs flow automatically into Cloud ALM implementation structures  ensuring every configuration task and project milestone is traceable to a specific process requirement

•  Test cases in Tricentis are associated with the Cloud ALM tasks they validate  and AI-driven impact analysis ensures that when anything changes, exactly the right tests run

•  Training content in Enable Now is built on Signavio process structures, synchronized automatically  users learn the actual designed process, not a months-old snapshot

•  WalkMe guidance in the live system operationalizes the process designs at the moment of execution  ensuring users follow the designed path in practice, not just in theory

•  WalkMe behavioral analytics and Enable Now usage data feed back into Signavio and LeanIX  surfacing adoption gaps, process deviations, and improvement opportunities that drive the next iteration

Notice that the Digital Thread is not linear  it's circular. The insights generated at the adoption end of the lifecycle (what users actually do in the live system) feed back into the architectural and process design decisions at the beginning of the next cycle. This is what transforms a one-time implementation project into a genuinely continuous improvement capability.

Every connection in that thread is a documented, out-of-the-box integration that SAP has built and supports. The question for every transformation program is simply: how much of the thread have you activated?

Who This Series Is For

This series is written for transformation practitioners in the broadest sense , people who are designing, governing, delivering, or supporting SAP transformation programs and who want to understand how to get significantly more value from the tools their organization has already invested in.

Specifically, I have the following readers in mind:

  • Enterprise Architects who want to understand how their architectural decisions in LeanIX can actively drive not just inform — every downstream activity in the transformation lifecycle, from process design through test execution and user adoption
  • Program Managers and PMO leads who are tired of spending program budget on manual coordination tasks between tools that should be talking to each other automatically, and who want real-time, integrated visibility across the full program without building it from scratch
  • Business Process Managers and Signavio practitioners who want to understand how their process designs can directly drive implementation tasks, test case design, and training content creating a single source of process truth that the entire program team works from
  • Digital Adoption leads working with WalkMe or Enable Now who want to understand how the adoption layer connects upward to the process and architectural layers and how behavioral data from the live system can feed back into process and architectural improvement cycles
  • Pre-Sales and Solution Architects who want to articulate the integrated value of the toolchain to customers with concrete, specific, outcome-focused language rather than product feature lists
  • SAP customers at any stage of their S/4HANA or Clean Core journey who suspect their toolchain could be working harder for them and want a structured way to evaluate where the Integration Gap is widest in their program

If you are new to the SAP Integrated Toolchain, this series is designed to bring you up to speed on what is possible. If you are already working with parts of the toolchain, it is designed to show you what you might be leaving on the table.

A Note on Sources and Setup Resources

Everything in this series is grounded in SAP's own documentation and my direct experience working with these integrations across customer programs in the ANZ region and globally. Where I describe specific integration capabilities, I am describing features that are available today  not roadmap items.

I will also flag, at the end of each blog, the  resources SAP has published to help teams set up each integration. This matters because knowing an integration exists and knowing how to configure it are two genuinely different things  and SAP has invested significantly in making the setup accessible.

The LeanIX Academy hosts dedicated video-based courses on the LeanIX–Signavio and LeanIX–Cloud ALM integrations. SAP Learning hosts courses on the full toolchain and on specific integration combinations. The Tricentis Academy offers free online training covering the Tricentis–Cloud ALM setup. The SAP Community hosts step-by-step configuration walkthroughs for the Signavio–Cloud ALM and Signavio–Solution Manager connectors. I will point you to the most directly relevant resources at the end of each blog  so that by the end of this series, you'll have both the strategic case for each integration and the practical starting point for activating it.

What Comes Next: Blog 1

We begin the integration deep-dives with Blog 1: LeanIX + SAP Cloud ALM  the integration that automates SAP landscape discovery and eliminates one of the most persistent pain points in Enterprise Architecture: keeping your application inventory accurate without spending half your time chasing down system owners for updates.

If you have ever inherited a LeanIX workspace where half the application fact sheets are out of date, or spent days building an application inventory from scratch before a transformation kick-off, Blog 1 will be directly relevant to your work.

Follow me on the SAP Community to receive each blog in the series as it publishes. If you have questions about any of the integrations covered here, or about the toolchain approach more broadly, feel free to reach out through the Community or through the Global Enterprise Architecture Advisory at SAP. I'm always glad to connect.

Let's close the Integration Gap — together.

Ramy Salem

ANZ Toolchain Ambassador at SAP

PhD Candidate – AI Agents for Enterprise Architecture & AI Adoption

Certified in SAP | WalkMe | LeanIX | Signavio |TOGAF

Read the 3 HOW'S Blogs  that inspired this series:

  • How Enterprise Architects Can Unlock Strategic Value with WalkMe in SAP Landscapes
  • How Enterprise Architects Can Unlock Strategic Value with SAP Signavio in SAP Landscapes
  • Bridging Strategy to Execution: How LeanIX, Signavio, and WalkMe Create the Ultimate EA Toolchain

entry.title: Re: Performance form, multiple managers
entry.content:
Hi BethGaston,   

For this scenario, we can use a Custom Role to represent the employee’s old manager. This relationship can be maintained in Job Relationships in Employee Profile. While configuring the route map, we can include this custom role as a step. As a result, both the old manager and the current manager will automatically have access to provide comments in the employee’s performance form.

entry.title: Control Key YBM1 YBM2 S4HANA Public Cloud
entry.content:
ear SAP Community,

We recently moved to SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition (Asset Management / Maintenance Orders) and are trying to understand the intended design for control keys YBM1 and YBM2.

What we understand from SAP documentation:

- YBM1 = internal processing (internal operations)

- YBM2 = external processing (external/vendor operations)

Our requirement:

We frequently use external vendors (offshore maintenance). Actual costs can vary (e.g., weather delays extend vendor time on board), so we want to use unplanned/limit services with an “overall limit” to avoid having to adjust the PR/PO each time the final amount changes.

How we used to do this (single PR):

1) Set the operation control key to YBM2 (external)

2) Add a service line within that same operation (Service tab / Service Overview)

3) Create an unplanned/limit service and maintain:

   - Expected value

   - Overall limit

Result: 1 purchase requisition line for the service.

 

What happens now in Public Cloud:

When we do the same, the system creates two PR lines:

- One PR line for the YBM2 external operation itself

- One PR line for the service line we entered on the operation

 

From our perspective, the PR line created for the operation is redundant because the service costs and limits are already maintained on the service line. We would like to understand what SAP’s intended process is here.

 

Questions:

1) Is this expected/standard behavior in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition when using YBM2 and planning services on the operation? (i read that it is, but that leaves the question; how do you connect a vendor contract/agreement number to the PR?)

2) What is the recommended best-practice to procure external services from maintenance orders while still using expected value / overall limit (and ideally avoiding redundant PR lines)? Please provide instructions on how YBM2 should be used when using “overall limit” services.

3) Is there a configuration or supported approach to suppress the “operation PR line” and keep only the service PR line?

 

Any guidance or references to the intended design would be greatly appreciated.

entry.title: Re: Logo in Manage Logos Not Appearing in Maintain E‑Mail...
entry.content:
Good day @Jessicaaguilar1 

Thank you for your question.

Based on the scenario you described, the key point in your case is the distinction between form branding and email body template content.

Note that the Manage Logos app is used to manage logos for master form templates (form output branding), not as a direct image repository for the Maintain Email Templates App. This is described in the following reference.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/a630d57fc5004c6383e7a81efee7a8bb/bd637484dc2f47a5a9071157f388ccd8.html?locale=en-US

The Maintain Email Templates app is used to maintain the email subject, which includes HTML content, but it does not provide a standard option you can directly reference or select a logo from Manage Logos. The app scope is email template maintenance rather than master form logo determination.

The following reference provides more details on how to maintain email templates.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/a630d57fc5004c6383e7a81efee7a8bb/cef4f21ba1f643318a9723b607aba838.html?locale=en-US

Question: How can I link or call the logo uploaded in Manage Logos so that it is displayed correctly in the e‑mail template?

Answer: Based on the information provided above, there is typically no direct link mechanism between Manage Logos and Maintain Email Templates for the email body HTML. The Manage Logos is intended for master form usage. See Manage Logos for more insights.

These Blogs provide guidance

S/4HANA Cloud Output Management – Customize Master Form for Logo and Texts (Footer Blocks)

Output Management – Customize Master Form for Logo and Address Tel Fax eMail

Question: Is there any additional configuration required in Output Management or in the template settings?

Answer: For the email body logo, Output Management configuration will usually not resolve this. The reason is, Output Management and Manage Logos are relevant for form output and master forms but not for editing email body.

Question: Are logos from Manage Logos supported in e‑mail templates, or do they need to be uploaded in a different way?

Answer: They are supported for master form that are based on output branding but not generally as a direct source for the Maintain Email Template HTML body.

Based on those details, I recommend the following:

  1. Clarify your target location of the logo (Email body and attached PDF/form output)
  2. If your requirement is email body logo, then maintain it in Body HTML with an HTML image reference, then test it in the receiving mail client. Also validate rendering in your Outlook or any other mail provider because external images may be blocked by client settings.
  3. If your requirement is PDF/form logo, then keep using Manage Logos and verify the master form that determines which logo is used for your specific business document.

To summarise:

  • Manage Logos is for master forms/form output branding.
  • Maintain Email Template is for email body but does not directly pull from Manage Logos.
  • Output Management config helps for form/PDF logo, not usually for email body logo.

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Spain Legal Form 303: Changes for 2026
entry.content:
The Spanish Tax Agency (AEAT) has published important updates to legal form 303 (VAT returns) in Order HAC/27/2026 on  January 26, 2026, that will take effect starting with the February 2026 reporting period. These changes introduce new fields and modify the TXT file format for electronic submissions.

  • December 5, 2025 - Draft law for 303 Adjustments published for public consultation
  • January 26, 2026 - Order HAC/27/2026 officially published in BOE
  • February 11, 2026 - Technical specifications for 01-1T (2026)
  • February 13, 2026 - Technical specifications for 02-12M and 2T-4T (2026)

In this post, the changes in the legal form 303 and the customizing will be explained.

 

Legal form adjustments

New Hydrocarbon sales and Deductions

According to this regulation, the following fields are included in teh VAT return:

  • New identification field: "Sujeto pasivo con derecho a deducir pago a cuenta de entregas de gasolinas, gasóleos y biocarburantes posteriores a la ultimación del régimen de depósito distinto del aduanero" (Tax payer eligible for deductions due to the pre payments for the delivery of gasolines and biodiesel after the extraction from Bonded Deposits).
  • New Tax box 112: "Pago a cuenta de entregas de gasolinas, gasóleos y biocarburantes posteriores a la ultimación del régimen de depósito distinto del aduanero atribuible a la Administración del Estado" (Pre-paid amounts for the delivery of gasolines and biodiesel after the extraction from Bonded Deposits)

 

Use case

The following example illustrates the process:

 

  • December:
    • The company A (importer company) imports 1.000L of fuel into the boded/tax warehouse. This transaction happens in a VAT suspension schema 
  • January
    • The company A sales to Company B (extractor company)  1.000L of fuel (this transaction is treated like an import):
      • Sales price: 1,5€/L
      • Tax base amount = 1.500€
      • VAT = 0€ 
    • Before the extraction. Assumption: company B is not registered as OEA (Operador Económico Autorizado, authorized Economic operator as defined in EU 952/2013) or Trustable Operator ("operador confiable" as defined in Orden HAC/1496/2025😞
      • Settle Import VAT with the Tax Agency (legal form 380): 
        • Import VAT (21%) = 1500€ x 21% =  315€.
      • Pre-payment by Company B to the Tax Agency (Legal form 319):  315€ x 110% = 346,50€.
    • After pre-payment:
      • Company A ensures that Company B either made the pre-payment or is identified  as Trustable Operator/OEA and authorizes the extraction. In case the extraction is authorized without this check, the Company A will be held as solidary responsible for the amount of 110% of the corresponding Import VAT.
      • Company B extracts the fuel from the bonded warehouse. 
        • Purchase price: 1,5€/L
        • Tax base amount = 1.500€
        • Import VAT (21%): 315€.
  • February
    • The company B sales 1000L to Company C (final customer).
      • Sales price: 1,7€/L
      • Tax base amount = 1.700€
      • Output VAT = 1700€ * 21% = 357€.
    • VAT return (303) by Company B
      • Output VAT = 1700€ * 21% = 357€
      • Pre-payment (tax box 112) = 346,50€
      • VAT return result = 357€ - 346,50€ = 10,5€ to pay to the Tax Agency.

 

Document and Reporting Compliance

New report configuration parameter ES_VAT_FUEL_DEDUCTION :

 

 The possible values are:

  • “0” = Not applicable (January and 1T, 2026)
  • “1” = Yes, taxpayer is eligible for deduction  
  • “2” =No, taxpayer is not eligible for deductions (as of February, 2T-4T 2026)

 

The taxbox 112 will be visible directly in DRC preview screen:

In case you need to notify a deduction in this tax box, you need to edit this legal form and change the value in the preview screen manually . You need to make sure that once the tax box is updated:

  • Return type is correct. For example, if you deduct all and the VAT return result is zero, the return type needs to be “N”.
  • Adjust Tax box 71 by subtracting the amount in tax box 112.
  • In case of corrections, adjust Tax box 111 with the newly calculated value of tax box 71 by subtracting the amount in tax box 112.

 

Tax Box 111 Calculation

The tax Agency has adjusted the calculation rules in case of rectification VAT return for tax box 111as follows:

  • Rectification VAT return = Yes
  • Tax Box [70] (Result to pay from previous returns of same year and period) is not zero.
  • Tax Box [71] (Final result ) is negative.
  • If Tax box[70] <= ABS(Tax box[71]),  then tax box [111] (Rectification amount other than discrepancy) = Tax box [70]
  • If Tax box[70] > ABS(Tax box[71]), then tax box [111] (Rectification amount other than discrepancy) = ABS(Tax box [71])

(Remark: ABS means absolute value, i.e.,  amount without sign)

TXT File Format Changes

The TXT file format has been restructured to accommodate the new fields.

  • The sequence of tax boxes [165] through [170] and [108] has been reordered within the file structure in records DP30301 and DP30303.
  • The CNAE values will change to 4 positions, instead of 3 in record structure DP30305.

 

Timeline

The regulation states in the final provision (disposicion final unica), that these changes will be applicable for the February reporting period:  "La presente orden…se aplicará por primera vez… a las autoliquidaciones del Impuesto sobre el Valor Añadido, modelos 303, 322 y 353 correspondientes al segundo trimestre o al mes de febrero de 2026".

Since the Tax Agency has activated the TXT format for January:

  • S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, the change is expected to be available on March 1st;. Due to the time constraints, in case of S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition you can check the note 716951 - TXT file for Legal form 303 for January 2026 rejected by the Tax Agency for additional support.
  • S/4HANA Cloud of Private Edition, the SAP Note 3717337 - ES_VAT_DCL_M303_NATIONAL - New format for reporting year 2026

Please stay tuned to the announcement Legal Form 303 (2026) in the Regulatory Change Manager.

Related Links

SAP remains committed to supporting customers through these regulatory changes, providing timely updates and comprehensive documentation to ensure smooth transitions and continued compliance. For technical questions or implementation assistance, please contact SAP Support or consult the relevant SAP Notes and documentation:

  • Document and Reporting Compliance in Public Cloud
  • Document and Reporting Compliance in Private Cloud

Disclaimer

This blog post is intended for informational purposes only and reflects a simplified configuration approach based on current legal and system understanding. It does not constitute legal or implementation advice. SAP system behavior may vary depending on version, scope, and customer-specific settings. For full compliance and implementation support, please consult your SAP implementation partner or legal advisor.

entry.title: Re: Side effect not working after deleting the child inst...
entry.content:
Hello, @AbhijeetT

The main problem is that the parent entity won't update automatically. You need to specify all triggers and declare a subscription to changes about the child entity in the parent entity.

Based on the problem you described, there is a solution. Below are example of BDEFs declaration and screenshots.

1. BDEF TP - here on the Header specify side effect to entity (line 14), and trigger condition for determination on the child entity (lines 26 - 31).

managed;
strict ( 2 );
with draft;

define behavior for ZI_AIL_JE_V3 alias Header
draft table zailjed3
//*//
{
//*//
association _GLItem { create; with draft; }

side effects
{
entity _GLItem affects field TotalBalance;

}
}

define behavior for ZI_AIL_GLIT_V3 alias GLItems
draft table zailglitd3
//*//
{
//*//
association _Header { with draft; }

determination calculatetotalbalance on modify { delete; field DebitAmountInCoCodeCrcy, CreditAmountInCoCodeCrcy; }

side effects
{
field DebitAmountInCoCodeCrcy affects field _Header.TotalBalance, field _Header.CriticalityTotalBalance;
field CreditAmountInCoCodeCrcy affects field _Header.TotalBalance, field _Header.CriticalityTotalBalance;

}
}

2. BDEF PROJECTION

projection;
strict ( 2 );
use draft;
use side effects;

define behavior for ZC_AIL_JE_V3
{
use create;
use update;
use delete;

use action Edit;
use action Resume;
use action Discard;
use action Activate;
use action Prepare;

use association _GLItem { create; with draft; }
}

define behavior for ZC_AIL_GLIT_V3
{
use update;
use delete;

use association _Header { with draft; }
}

3. Result on UI 
#Before delete Item#After delete Item

If you have any questions or problems, please let me know.

Best regards,
Aliaksandr.

entry.title: Re: SAC Planning and Datasphere: How to use Audit Logs for Analysis and Recovery

Hey @Tano_B ,

Really cool post! 

No SAC expert myself, so forgive me if I ask a stupid question or two 🙂 .

  1. In BW, the REQUEST TSN is just a timestamp, although a very detailed one.
    In the Data Change log, can the combination of [Audit Time, Audit User] not be considered a "unique" transaction, similar to a request in an ADSO?
  2. Because we can plan on nodes in a hierarchy, how would you suggest we handle something like rolling back a single transaction?

    Should we then roll back all transactions from my mistake and forward?

Again, just curious! Really love the blog and the idea 🙂

entry.title: Re: AUTO update MIGO date into MIRO baseline date
entry.content:
Dear @Gautam_FI,

In classic MIRO (on-prem / private cloud) the Badi you would use to set Baseline Date (ZFBDT) based on GR/MIGO posting date is:

  • Badi MRM_PAYMENT_TERMS it can change ZFBDT and SAP even describes the example baseline date derived from goods receipt date

However this is a classic MM-IV Badi and is not provided as a released Cloud Badi for key user/developer extensibility in Public Cloud

In Public Cloud apps SAP standard behavior is:

  • baseline date is proposed according to payment terms customizing in CBC SSCUI 102934 Maintain Terms of Payment

SAP Note below:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3457995

  • SAP does not describe any released Badi there to switch baseline date logic to GR posting date

So you cannot implement Baseline Date = MIGO/GR posting date via a Public Cloud Badi for normal supplier invoice entry

IMPORTANT:

SAP does provide a Cloud Badi for ERS:

  • MRM_ERS_DATA_MODIFY_CE

SAP Note below:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3383434

But that’s only for ERS not for manual PO invoice posting in Create Supplier Invoice / MIRO logic

Practical options in Public Cloud:

1. Configuration only (standard) adjust payment terms baseline date rule in SSCUI 102934 but it won’t give you GR date

2. Process change so use ERS then you have a Cloud Badi available

3. Integration approach post invoices via API/integration where you control baseline date (clean-core), if business can accept that

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Re: Absolute Exchange Rate Not Visible in Open Items in M...
entry.content:
Good day @Jessicaaguilar1 

Thank you for your question.

Based on your screenshots, it matches what the system is expected to do. Below are answers to your questions.

Question: Why is the absolute exchange rate not available as a column in the open items view?

Answer: The short answer is that Manage Supplier Line Items only displays the fields that are exposed by app data source and UI configuration. The app is intended for supplier line item operations (open items, clearing and payment related process) and not all journal entry attributes are available as list columns. You can find more information in Manage Supplier Line Items: FAQ.       

So, even though the Journal Entry object contains the Absolute Exchange Rate as you have in your drilldown (attached screenshot), that filed is not necessarily exposed in the Manage Supplier Line Items table.

Question: Is this the standard behaviour of the app?

Answer: yes, it is a standard behaviour if, the field does not appear in column personalisation, Adapt Filters, and there are no standard settings in the app to enable it. In other words, the filed exists in the accounting document but the app list view is not designed to display it.

Question: Is there any way to display it without having to open each journal entry individually?

Answer: The short answer is no, not if the filed is not exposed as a column.

You may consider using a different report focused on journal entries to analyse posting attributes, build a custom report and check key user extensibility. See the following references for more details.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/031c345485b84c8c94265be9ef61d3a8/86248754a0cd9d62e10000000a445394.html?locale=en-US

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/031c345485b84c8c94265be9ef61d3a8/16faf6b93de44a4cadce11f9094ef06e.html?locale=en-US data source extensions

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Re: Customer Specific Inventory Reservation based on Bill...
entry.content:
Contracts in SAP (VA41/VA42) are agreements for future sales, not physical stock reservations. They don’t actually block or segregate inventory; they only ensure that sales orders reference the contract. So while they can control pricing/quantities for a specific customer, they cannot reserve stock in the warehouse or prevent other customers from consuming it.

If consignment is not acceptable, your Z‑table + user exit approach is valid, but to simplify:

  • Use storage location control (special storage location for reserved stock).
  • Add a simple enhancement in ATP check (user exit EXIT_SAPLATPC_001) to prioritize reserved stock for the mapped Bill‑to party.
  • For expiry, a batch job can move expired reserved stock back to unrestricted (movement 311). This way, you avoid complex development and keep it lightweight.

The other option is below one

Maintain a variable table (TVARVC) with entries like:

  • Bill‑To Party
  • Material
  • Reserved Quantity
  • Valid From / Valid To

This table acts as the “reservation master.” Business users can update it directly without ABAP changes.

Logic Flow

1) Sales Order Creation (VBAP / VBEP check)

  • When a sales order line is created, trigger a user exit (e.g., USEREXIT_CHECK_VBAP or ATP check exit EXIT_SAPLATPC_001).
  • Read TVARVC for a matching entry:
    • Bill‑To = Sales Order Bill‑To
    • Material = Sales Order Material
    • Validity period covers current date

2) Stock Confirmation

  • If a match is found:
    • Confirm stock from the reserved storage location (dedicated for this scenario).
    • Reduce the reserved quantity in TVARVC (or maintain consumption logic in a Z‑table linked to TVARVC).
  • If reserved quantity is insufficient:
    • Confirm the available reserved stock first.
    • Remaining quantity is confirmed from unrestricted stock.

3) Expiry Handling

  • A background job checks TVARVC daily.
  • If Valid To < today, then:
    • Move any remaining reserved stock back to unrestricted (movement 311).
    • Clear or deactivate the TVARVC entry.

4) Visibility Control

  • For other customers (Bill‑To not matching TVARVC entry):
    • Reserved storage location stock is invisible for ATP.
    • Only unrestricted stock is considered.

entry.title: Re: SAC Planning and Datasphere: How to use Audit Logs for Analysis and Recovery
entry.content:
@CLTGravesen really appreciate your questions!
I would say, to be 100% sure, you should look at the combination of Timestamp, User and Version, because you can only post to a specific version with a single data action to invert postings. When you publish data, you can publish to more than one version at once, so you could have data on different versions at the same timestamp.

Regarding your second question, it denpends:

  • If you never had 1 Million on the correct accounts, you have to 1. undo the disaggregation and 2. undo the deletion of the two accounts and 3. plan the 1 Million afterwards.
  • If you had the 1 Million already planned correctly before, it would be sufficient to undo disaggregation and the deletion

entry.title: Re: Fix pdfmake Table Width Issues with Long Continuous Text and Special Characters (SAP UI5)
entry.content:
Great write-up—this is a classic works in testing, breaks in production scenario.

The key insight here is that it’s not really a column width issue, but a line-breaking limitation. When you feed pdfmake long, continuous strings with no natural breakpoints, the layout engine simply has nowhere to wrap—so it overflows, even with fixed widths.

I especially appreciate that you focused on a practical workaround that preserves the visual output without redesigning the layout. Real-world data always exposes edge cases like this, and documenting them is incredibly valuable.

entry.title: Re: Record quantity indicator in center cost
entry.content:
Good day @jantoniomoyano3 

Thank you for your question.

Your question is very generic. Based on your description, what you experienced is expected. I will address your it as follows:

Question: Is there any information about this field or how it works?

Answer: Yes, but it is often not explained in a very visible way in Public Cloud app level help.

In practice, the Record Quantity field in the cost center is a control indicator for quantity recording behaviour. It does not directly control amount posting logic and does not automatically create an amount warning by itself.

The reason why you do not see differences in your posting when the flag is marked or unmarked can be linked to a few factors such as:

  • Your posting scenario is not quantity relevant
  • Your posting account is not set up for quantity recording
  • Your process does not trigger interactive warning checks
  • You are comparing only amounts. This field usually does not change amount postings.

So, the lack of visible difference in your posted documents does not necessarily mean the field is not working.

Regarding the lack of warning about the amount, this is usually not the case, not specifically about the amount. This field is generally related to quantity entry/quantity recording checks, not amount tolerance or amount validation. If your expectation is an amount warning, that is typically controlled elsewhere, not by the by the Record Quantity indicator on the cost center.

You can test it by using a posting scenario that supports quantity entry, use a quantity relevant posting account, post once with quantity and once without and compare the warnings. See the factors mentioned above.

I recommend you verify in your system that:

  • The cost flag is activated in Manage Cost Centers
  • Posting account is quantity relevant
  • Test in posting app that supports manual quantity entry, compare message behaviour and quantity fields in journal entry.

See the following references for more details.

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/2752349

This Blog provides details: Custom Journal Entry Query by G/L and Cost Center in S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Questions and Answers about E-Invoicing and E-Reporting for France in SAP Business One
entry.content:
On December 29, 2023, the French authorities published legislation in the 2024 budget that included updated timelines for the e-invoicing and e-reporting mandate. According to this legislation, the implementation schedule is as follows:

September 1, 2026: All taxpayers will be required to receive e-invoices. Additionally, the issuance of B2B (business-to-business) e-invoices and e-reporting will become mandatory for large enterprises ("GE") and intermediate-sized enterprises ("ETI").

September 1, 2027: The issuance of B2B e-invoices and e-reporting will become mandatory for small and medium-sized enterprises ("PME") and micro companies ("TPE").

SAP is officially registered as an Agreed Platform (PA) by the French tax authorities, the General Directorate of Public Finance (DGFiP). This registration enables customers to seamlessly comply with France's e-invoicing and e-reporting requirements.

In SAP Business One, the functionality that will support the E-Invoicing and E-Reporting in France is under development. Since partners have been asking many questions in advance, this blog post aims to provide clarity and serve as a helpful resource for all partners and customers who may have similar inquiries.

The summary of the questions and answers can be found here.

Q1:

Will the agreed platform (PA), the mandatory third party in France, be SAP?

Answer

SAP Document and Reporting Compliance (DRC), Cloud Edition, will be supported as an approved platform (PA) by SAP Business One. SAP DRC, Cloud Edition, is a platform approved by the French tax authorities. If customers want to use the end-to-end solution, they will have to subscribe to DRC. DRC will be able to receive electronic documents in all three formats (UBL, CII, and Factur-X) and will transform them into UBL format in order to import these electronic documents into SAP Business One.

Q2:

Regarding imports, will the purchase invoice be created in SAP Business One with links to the goods receipt PO?

Answer

Yes, it is possible to link A/P invoice to either a goods receipt PO or a purchase order.

Q3:

Regarding export, will a wizard be used?

Answer

The Document Generation Wizard can be used for this purpose.

Q4:

Will SAP provide a DRC connector for SAP Business One, version for SAP HANA and SAP Business One SQL?

Answer:

It will be delivered for both. It is universal.

Q5:

How do the connections work between DRC SaaS and SAP Business One On-Premise?

Answer:

This part is under development. All details will be provided once completed.

Q6:

SAP has been registered as PA by French local authorities. How will our customers be able to use/subscribe to this service?

Answer:

Please find the link to the SAP help page where the onboarding process is explained in more detail:

https://help.sap.com/docs/cloud-edition/sap-document-and-reporting-compliance-cloud-edition/getting-started-with-integration-of-process

Q7:

What components or configurations must we implement? Is there a specific solution or SAP module to which the customer needs to subscribe?

Answer:

After customers complete onboarding for DRC Cloud Edition, they can activate the process: https://help.sap.com/docs/cloud-edition/sap-document-and-reporting-compliance-cloud-edition/activating-processes?locale=en-US 

They need to integrate Peppol Exchange with SAP Business One: https://help.sap.com/docs/cloud-edition/sap-document-and-reporting-compliance-cloud-edition/integrating-peppol-exchange-with-sap-business-one?locale=en-US&q=France+process  and activate processes for France.

How the connection between DRC and SAP  Business One will work is still under development. All details will be announced once development is complete.

Q8:

The SIREN, SIRET, Routing Code, Suffix are required as part of the electronic address. Where can we find them?

Answer:

The SIREN number is available in Business Partner Master Data → Accounting tab → Tax sub-tab.

SIRET number, Routing Code and Suffix are available in Business Partner Master Data → Address tab → Bill To address.

SAP Business One plans to support basic scenarios. The list can be found here:

Additional mappings need to be made by the relevant partner and/or customer to support other industry-specific or use-case-specific scenarios.

Many of open issues might be clarified in this work zone: https://workzone.one.int.sap/site#workzone-home&/groups/CK8AhwChqYjMisy8oYSfgv/workpage_tabs/LxnjcOht6DIhuitgncZLhH 

I hope this Q&A brings more clarity to this topic. Should you have any further questions, please share them in the comment section

entry.title: Advanced Intercomapny Stock Transfer scenario and TM authorizations
entry.content:
Advanced stage building is called for advanced intercompany STO scenario for FU creation in TM whether you activate it on integration profile or not. (rlv. class. /SCMTMS/CL_FUB_STG_BUILD_ADV)

Based on entered incoterm and incoterm location 1, FU stages may be split and relevant organizations (exec org and paying org) can be assigned to stage level. In some scenarios and combinations, both stages could be assigned different involved companies (sender and receiver).

In this type of situation, FU stages may be planned by involved organizations to different capacity documents. It means that FU needs to be available to pass both organizations authorization checks.

Question is; does T_TOR_EXE authorization object checks (in worklists, doc UIs, TC, etc.) only works for root execution organization or does it also check the stage execution organization?

PRD. Version: SAP S/4HANA 2023 FPS03 (on premise)

Thanks in advance.
